question: Prove: Sam is bitter.
label: yes
generated_excerpt:
<think>
We are asked to show that Sam is bitter. The context provides a network of properties linking Sam to other entities and to the predicate bitter, so we will follow the chain of implications step by step.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a dumpus, then Sam is bitter.</text>
<logic>bitter(sam) :- dumpus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a numpus, then Sam is a dumpus.</text>
<logic>dumpus(sam) :- numpus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a vumpus, then Sam is a numpus.</text>
<logic>numpus(sam) :- vumpus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a lempus, then Sam is a vumpus.</text>
<logic>vumpus(sam) :- lempus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a rompus, then Sam is a lempus.</text>
<logic>lempus(sam) :- rompus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is an impus, then Sam is a rompus.</text>
<logic>rompus(sam) :- impus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a brimpus, then Sam is a lempus.</text>
<logic>lempus(sam) :- brimpus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a gorpus, then Sam is a brimpus.</text>
<logic>brimpus(sam) :- gorpus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a lorpus, then Sam is a gorpus.</text>
<logic>gorpus(sam) :- lorpus(sam)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="premise">
<text>If Sam is a wumpus, then Sam is a lorpus.</tex

title: "Unknown" on Binary Task

question: Prove: Polly is not small.
label: yes
generated_excerpt:
<think>
We are asked to show that Polly is not small. The context provides a network of properties linking dumpuses, lorpus, brimpuses, numpuses, and Polly, so we must chain these implications to reach the desired negation.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>Any dumpus is luminous.</text>
<logic>luminous(polly) :- dumpus(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>Any lorpus is a sterpus.</text>
<logic>sterpus(polly) :- lorpus(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>Any brimpus is an impus.</text>
<logic>impus(polly) :- brimpus(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>Any impus is moderate.</text>
<logic>moderate(polly) :- impus(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>Any numpus is a brimpus.</text>
<logic>brimpus(polly) :- numpus(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>Any sterpus is not small.</text>
<logic>~small(polly) :- sterpus(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>Polly is a lorpus.</text>
<logic>lorpus(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="derived" from="7,2">
<text>Since Polly is a lorpus and every lorpus is a sterpus, Polly is a sterpus.</text>
<logic>sterpus(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived" from="8,6">
<text>Because Polly is a sterpus and any sterpus is not small, Polly is not small.</text>
<logic>~small(polly)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="derived" from="7,5">
<text>Polly is a lorpus, and any lorpus is a numpus, so Polly is a numpus.

title: Hallucinated Premises

question: The cat is not big.
label: false
generated_excerpt:
<think>
We are asked to decide whether the cat is not big. To answer this, we must see if the knowledge base can derive that the cat is big, which would make the negation false.

<step n="1" type="premise">
<text>If someone visits the bald eagle and they are green then they visit the lion.</text>
<logic>visits_lion(bald_eagle) :- visits_bald_eagle(bald_eagle), green(bald_eagle)</logic>
</step>

<step n="2" type="premise">
<text>If someone sees the lion and they eat the lion then they are big.</text>
<logic>big(cat) :- sees_lion(cat), eats_lion(cat)</logic>
</step>

<step n="3" type="premise">
<text>If someone sees the cat and they see the mouse then the mouse eats the cat.</text>
<logic>eats_cat(mouse) :- sees_cat(mouse), sees_mouse(mouse)</logic>
</step>

<step n="4" type="premise">
<text>The bald eagle is green.</text>
<logic>green(bald_eagle)</logic>
</step>

<step n="5" type="premise">
<text>The cat is blue.</text>
<logic>blue(cat)</logic>
</step>

<step n="6" type="premise">
<text>The lion is blue.</text>
<logic>blue(lion)</logic>
</step>

<step n="7" type="premise">
<text>The cat sees the lion.</text>
<logic>sees_lion(cat)</logic>
</step>

<step n="8" type="premise">
<text>The lion sees the bald eagle.</text>
<logic>sees_bald_eagle(lion)</logic>
</step>

<step n="9" type="derived" from="4,8,1">
<text>Because the lion sees the bald eagle and the bald eagle is green, the lion visits the bald eagle.</text>
<logic>visits_bald_eagle(lion)</logic>
</step>

<step n="10" type="derived" from="9,3">
<text>Since the lion visits the bald eagle and the lion sees the mouse, the lion eats the mouse.</text>
<logic>eats_mouse(lion)</logic>
</step>

<step n="11" type="derived" from="10,7">
<text>Given that the lion eats the mouse and the lion sees the cat, the lion eats the cat.</text>
<logic>eats_cat(lion)</logic>
</step>

<step n="12" type="derived" from="11,2">
<text>Because the lion eats the cat and the lion sees the cat, the lion is big.</text>
<logic>big(lion)</logic>
</s
